attributes.stem:
A 33-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a two-day history of increasing pain in her left breast. She gave birth to her third child 12 days ago via an uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal delivery. She has had good breastmilk production and has continued to feed from both breasts alternately. She denies dysuria, abdominal pain, or change in the appearance or smell of her lochia. Her medical history is unremarkable and she takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears uncomfortable. Her pulse rate is 115/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 19/min and temperature 37.3°C. The left breast is swollen with erythema over the lateral aspect, which is warm on palpation. There is a clear/white nipple discharge. The right breast has a normal appearance.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate advice regarding breastfeeding from the left breast?
options: Express from the left breast but discard the milk
Continue to breastfeed from the left as normal
Temporarily discontinue the breastfeeding and expressing on the left
Breastfeed more frequently from the left
Actively compress the breast during feeding
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with signs and symptoms consistent with early mastitis, which is inflammation and swelling of the milk ducts causing milk to accumulate and infiltrate into surrounding breast tissue. The most appropriate management in this case is to continue to breastfeed from the left as normal. Continuing to breastfeed helps to empty the breast, reducing engorgement and preventing further blockage of milk ducts.

Expressing from the left breast but discarding the milk is unnecessary. In early mastitis without signs of infection, the milk is safe for the baby and should not be discarded. Expressing can be helpful if the baby is unable to feed effectively, but the milk can still be given to the baby.

Temporarily discontinuing the breast-feeding and expressing on the left would likely worsen the condition. Stopping breastfeeding can lead to further milk accumulation, increasing pain and the risk of progression to more severe mastitis or breast abscess.

Breast-feeding more frequently from the left is not necessarily wrong, but it's not the most appropriate first-line advice. While frequent feeding can help, it's more important to ensure regular and complete emptying of both breasts rather than favouring the affected side.

Actively compressing the breast during feeding is not recommended as a first-line approach. While gentle massage can be helpful, active compression might cause pain and potentially worsen the condition.

In addition to continuing normal breastfeeding, other important measures for managing early mastitis include applying warm compresses before feeding to help with milk flow, ensuring proper latch and positioning of the baby, and gently massaging the breast towards the nipple during feeding. If symptoms persist or worsen, or if the patient develops fever or systemic symptoms, antibiotics may be necessary.

It's important to note that mastitis can often be prevented by regular, frequent breastfeeding alternating between breasts, ensuring proper latch and positioning, and avoiding tight-fitting bras or clothing that might compress the breasts. Early recognition and management of symptoms is crucial to prevent progression to more severe infection or breast abscess.
learningPoints: Patients with early lactation mastitis should be encouraged to continue breastfeeding.
Early mastitis can be managed with frequent feeding, heat or cold therapy, and gentle massage.
Mastitis can be prevented by regular, frequent breastfeeding alternating between breasts.
references: Guideline on Breastfeeding support and promotion. In: The Royal Children's Hospital, Melbourne, Australia, Clinical Practice [online]. Accessed 2020 Aug 8. Available at: https://wwww.rch.org.au/clinicalguide/
Mastitis. In: The Royal Women’s Hosptial [online]. Accessed 2020 Aug 8. Available at: https://www.thewomens.org.au/health-information/breastfeeding/breastfeeding-problems/mastitis.
Mastitis. In: Australian Breastfeeding Association [online]. Accessed 2020 Aug 8. Available at: https://www.breastfeeding.asn.au/bf-info/common-concerns–mum/mastitis

Boakes E, Woods A, Johnson N, Kadoglou. Breast Infection: A Review of Diagnosis and Management Practices. Eur J Breast Health 2018;14:136-143. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6092150/
